text: Light.
Too much light.
Your eyes have never done this before. Not really. Every photon hits like a small fist.
You were born an hour ago. You are thirty-six years old.
The ceiling is rust and wire and something dripping somewhere.
A man is looking down at you. Morpheus. He looks exactly like you always knew he would.
He says nothing. He is watching you decide.

choices.label: Force your eyes open. Take it all in.
Squeeze them shut. It's too much.

text: You keep them open.
It burns. A slow, specific burn behind both eyes.
The light does not stop being what it is.
But after a minute, you can see edges. After two, shapes.
Tank — a large man with a warm face and no jacks in his skull — leans in.
"Look at that. Most people need a week before they stop flinching."
Morpheus nods once. Something passes across his face. Not pride. Something quieter than that.
Acknowledgment.
You file it away. Even now. Even in a body you have never trusted.
You notice things.
You shut them.
Tank moves without being asked. The work lights dim. Something rustles — a cloth draped over the nearest panel.
"Take your time," Tank says. "The dark is fine. The dark is honest."
You breathe. Real air. Air with iron in it, with rot, with the smell of old machines.
In the Matrix, air smelled like nothing. Like the absence of a question.
This air has an opinion.
You open your eyes slowly, into the managed dark.
And in the dimness, you see something no one else noticed.
A number. Scratched into the bulkhead above you. 2-1-3. Someone was here before you.
Someone left a mark.

choices.label: Remember that number. Then try to sit up.

text: Your muscles are not yours.
They are borrowed things. Ninety-nine percent atrophied. The real word for what they are is "vestigial."
You try to sit up.
Your arms do not comply. They shake and then they stop shaking and then they shake again.
You land on your side. The metal floor is cold.
Tank doesn't flinch. He's seen this before. He knows exactly what this is.
Morpheus has not moved.
He watches you the way someone watches a river — you can't rush it, but you cannot look away.

choices.label: Accept Tank's help. Let him lift you.

text: His hands are steady. That matters more than you expected.
He lifts you as though he's done this before. He has. They all come through here the same way.
You sit on the edge of the platform. Your feet dangle. Your legs are the legs of someone who doesn't know you.
"Good," Tank says. "The first one who won't let me help usually cracks a rib."
Morpheus finally speaks.
"There is no shame in the body being what it is," he says. "Shame belongs to what they did with it."
You look at your hands.
They are white. The color of something that has never seen sun.
Real hands. For the first time. Real.

choices.label: Ask Tank for food. You're starving.

text: You try again.
Tank watches with his hands ready.
You make it to an elbow. Then a wrist. Then something in your left shoulder seizes and you gasp.
The sound that comes out of you is not a sound you've made before.
Raw. Honest. Animal.
Morpheus says: "Pain is data. Use it."
It takes nine minutes.
Nine minutes to sit up from a floor you've been on for thirty seconds.
But when you get there, you got there.
Tank hands you a cup of water without commentary.
The water is the best thing you have ever tasted.
The water is grey.
Tank slides a bowl across the floor toward you.
It is grey. Thick. The texture of something that was never meant to be looked at.
"Protein. Vitamins. Everything you need." He pauses. "Nothing you'd want."
Morpheus looks at the far wall. He gives you the moment.
The bowl smells like nothing. Like engineered absence.

choices.label: Eat it. Whatever it is, you need it.
"Not yet." You need to ask something first.

text: You eat it.
It is everything Tank said. Nothing more.
You eat it the way you've been asleep: without understanding it, just completing the act.
Halfway through the bowl, Tank says quietly: "Twenty-six years, by the way."
You stop chewing.
"That's how long you were in."
The spoon makes a small sound against the bowl.
You put it down carefully.
Like it might break.
Like you might.

choices.label: Go silent. You can't process more right now.

text: "How long?"
Tank looks at Morpheus. Morpheus gives him the smallest nod.
"Twenty-six years," Tank says. "Give or take."
The bowl of grey mush is still there.
The number sits in the air between you.
You look at your hands again. The white, atrophied hands.
Wasted. All of it wasted — building to nothing, working for something that was never real.
You pick up the bowl.
You eat.
It tastes like surrender and like starting over simultaneously.
You eat every drop.

choices.label: Go silent. Sit with the number.

text: Tank talks for a long time.
He talks about pods. Millions of them. Billions.
He talks about what they do with the energy. What they do with the dead.
He does not soften it.
You asked for everything. He gives you everything.
At some point Morpheus sits.
He lets Tank carry this. Tank was born in the real world. Tank knows it in his body the way you know it in yours now — the way a cold floor teaches you things school cannot.
When Tank is done, the ship is quiet.
You don't ask any more questions.
You have them. But they can wait.
Everything can wait, for the next few minutes, while you let this land.
You say nothing.
Tank respects this.
Morpheus respects this.
The ship breathes around you — ducts and vents and the low-frequency hum of something perpetually moving.
You sit with the number.
Twenty-six years.
The weight of it is not in the time lost. It's in what you were building — the career, the relationships, the morning routines — and what any of it actually was.
Architecture built on air.
You lived inside an argument about reality and thought you were the argument's author.
After a long time you look up.
"I want to see myself," you say.
It comes out very steady.
You don't know where you found steady.
Tank finds a piece of polished metal. It is not quite a mirror. It is close enough.
You hold it up.
The person looking back is not Thomas Anderson.
Thomas Anderson was tan. Had muscle memory from eight years of bad gym habits. Had a specific crease at the corner of his left eye that appeared when he was thinking hard.
This person is pale. The color of paper held up to light.
The eye-crease is gone. Every expression you practiced — gone.
What is left is something underneath. The structure beneath the performance.
Cheekbones. The line of a jaw. Eyes that are — and this is the strange thing — sharper than Thomas Anderson's ever were.
Like something that was always there, finally allowed to surface.
textPrompt: You are looking at your real face for the first time. What do you say?

text: Whatever you say, Tank hears it.
He nods like it makes sense.
Maybe it does. Maybe everyone who comes through here says the exact right thing and the exact wrong thing simultaneously.
Morpheus stands.
"There is one question left," he says. "Before we enter the construct."
He looks at you the way teachers look at students who have been asking better questions than expected.
"You can ask me anything. One thing, before you go in."
He waits.

choices.label: "What happens if you die in there?"

text: "Anything," Morpheus says.
He says it the way mathematicians say "infinity" — like the word is a door and the room behind it has no ceiling.
"The rules of physics — they are not laws there. They are suggestions. And you do not have to follow suggestions."
"Anything you believe strongly enough becomes possible."
He pauses.
"That is also the danger."
Tank fires up the loading program.
The chair is ready.
White light fills the room.

choices.label: Close your eyes. Enter the construct.

text: Morpheus pauses.
The pause is long enough that you start to wonder if you asked the wrong question.
Then: "Nothing you don't believe is impossible."
He lets that sit.
"The construct operates on conviction. The physics defer to the mind. But if your mind already knows a thing cannot be done — the construct agrees with your mind."
"The ceiling is not what the program sets," he says. "The ceiling is what you carry in."
Tank starts the program.
You file that away.
You are already thinking about the ceiling.
Morpheus doesn't look away.
"The body cannot survive without the mind," he says.
"If you die in the Matrix — die believing you are dead — your heart stops. Here. In the chair. On the ship."
Tank has gone very still behind the monitor.
"The Matrix is not a game. It will not tell you when you've lost."
Morpheus holds your gaze.
"I am telling you this because you asked, and because you deserve to know what you are walking into."
A beat.
"Are you still ready?"
The chair is waiting.
The program is loaded.
The dojo is yours. All of it.
Morpheus comes through the door and doesn't announce himself.
He is in a fighting stance before you register he has moved.
You attack first.
This is your first mistake.
He catches your leading strike with a palm — no effort, no adjustment.
Then you are looking at the ceiling.
You hit the mat hard enough that the air leaves your body in a single indignant burst.
"Again," he says.

choices.label: Get up angry. You want to hit him harder.
Get up humbled. Watch how he stands before you move.

text: You get up fast.
Too fast. Your footing is wrong. Your chin is out.
Morpheus watches you come and does nothing until the last possible second.
You land on your back again. Harder this time.
"Stop leading with your pride," he says.
You lie there staring at the construct ceiling.
There is no sky here. Just white.
White that says: you are still learning.
You breathe.
The anger doesn't leave. But something shifts under it.
Something that is not anger but lives near anger and has better posture.
You get up differently this time.

choices.label: Watch him. Actually watch. Then move.

text: You get up slowly.
You don't charge.
You watch the way he holds his weight — forward on the left, loaded on the right.
You watch the way his shoulders telegraph before his hands.
He lets you watch.
When you move again, you move like someone who has gathered information.
You still don't land a clean strike.
But he uses more of his range to stop you.
"Better," he says.
The word is small. It is everything.

choices.label: Ask for more programs. Push the session.

text: The guns appear in your hands before you ask for them.
You know how to use them. The knowledge sits in your wrists, your stance, your grip.
It feels borrowed. It is borrowed.
But it works.
Tank loads another sequence. And another. And another.
After the ninth sequence, he pauses.
"That's... probably enough for today."
You look at him.
"One more."
He looks at Morpheus.
Morpheus looks at you.
There is a question in his expression.
He loads it.
You absorb it.
When Tank finally pulls the plug it's because you've been in the chair for sixteen hours and your real body's vitals are starting to dip.
You come out of the construct gasping.
Not from pain. From the shock of being in a body again.
Tank hands you water. His face has the expression of a person who respects something they don't quite understand.
Morpheus has not moved from the corner.
"You are almost ready," he says.
"Almost?"
"There is one more thing you need."
He nods toward the door.
"The rooftop."
Two buildings. A gap between them — sixty feet of nothing.
Wind. The smell of the construct's simulated city.
Morpheus stands at the edge of the far roof.
He jumped to get there. You saw it. It didn't look possible.
"Free your mind," he says.
The gap is very wide.
You take five steps back.
You breathe.
You stand at the edge.
Your legs don't move.
You tell them to move. They consider it. They decline.
You look down.
That's the second mistake. Looking down.
Morpheus watches from the other side.
"The body knows what the mind refuses," he says.
You try twice more.
You fall short on the third attempt.
The construct catches you. You don't hit the ground. You just — stop. Suspended. Reset.
The gap feels bigger than before.
You learn something today. Not the lesson they planned.
You learn what it feels like to let your mind stop your body.
You will not do that again.

choices.label: Remember this. Go see the Oracle.

text: You run.
You don't think about the gap.
You don't think about the drop.
You think about the other side and then you stop thinking entirely.
Your feet leave the edge.
There is a moment.
A single moment in which you are entirely in the air and the world has not yet decided what to do with you.
You land on the far roof.
You land badly — knees, palms, a sharp crack at your left shoulder.
But you land.
Morpheus looks at you for a long time.
"Most people don't make it their first attempt."
He reaches down. Helps you up.
"You did."

choices.label: "What happens when I'm out there and I can't not-think?"

text: You're back in the construct. A hallway. Standard.
Tank is running a navigation sequence.
And then — a door glitches.
Not dramatically. Not like a crash. More like a hiccup.
For a fraction of a second the door is a door and something else simultaneously.
Numbers. A cascade of them. Green and structured and deeply, completely real.
You see the code beneath the simulation.
And then it's a door again.
Your heart is doing something interesting.
Morpheus is twenty feet away, not looking.

choices.label: Say nothing. Study the door on your own.

text: "Morpheus."
He turns.
"The door. It — something happened to it. For a second I could see..."
You describe it. He listens without interrupting.
When you finish, he is quiet for what feels like too long.
Then: "What color was it?"
"Green."
He looks at the door. Then at you.
"Some see it early," he says. "Most never see it at all."
He doesn't say more than that.
He doesn't need to.
You are both looking at the door like it's said something they can't take back.

choices.label: Go see the Oracle. You're as ready as you'll be.

text: You stand in front of the door.
You look at it the way a programmer looks at unfamiliar code.
It doesn't glitch again.
But you stand there long enough that Tank finally calls over the comms: "You okay in there?"
"Fine."
"You've been staring at a door for eleven minutes."
You file it. The pattern. The hesitation in the simulation's skin.
You will remember this.
You don't know yet that remembering it will matter more than anything else you've downloaded today.
Morpheus enters.
He doesn't announce himself.
You are already in position.
Not because you anticipated him — because you've been standing here for twenty minutes, studying the room.
The weight distribution of the mat. The way the light falls. The width of the space between you and the wall.
He attacks.
You don't attack back.
You move.
Specifically and exactly: you move to where he isn't.
You move again.
And again.
And on the fourth movement he stops.
He is looking at you with something new in his expression.
He missed.
Not because he lost focus.
Because you were already somewhere else.

choices.label: "I watched how you weighted your strikes."

text: Morpheus is quiet for a long time.
"You have been training for two days," he says.
"I know."
"No one reads me in two days."
"I wasn't reading you. I was reading the room."
He tilts his head slightly. The specific tilt of a question that has no prepared answer.
"Show me what you saw."
You walk him through it. The mat, the light, the angle of entry.
He listens the way a musician listens to someone hum a melody they've never heard before.
Carefully. With hunger.

choices.label: Ask Tank to replay the combat at quarter speed.

text: Tank runs the footage back.
Quarter speed. The whole sparring session rendered in slow motion.
You watch it twice.
And there it is.
A delay. Between Morpheus's intent and the construct's rendering of his body's movement.
Forty milliseconds. Maybe a little more.
Not long. But consistent.
Every strike. Every pivot. The same lag.
"Tank," you say. "Is that normal?"
Tank leans in and looks.
Then he looks at you.
"I... actually don't know," he says.
You write it down.
You call it the physics gap.

choices.label: Keep going. Ask Tank for a weapons download.

text: Tank's hand hovers over the program panel.
"Weapons package? Fifty programs, twelve disciplines. Most people load it day one."
You think about it.
Not the weapons themselves. What they represent.
Loading the weapons means trusting the knowledge. Using borrowed conviction.
You've been building your own.
"Not yet," you say. "I want to understand the rules before I start using them."
Morpheus looks at you sharply.
"The rules are not something you understand. They are something you overcome."
"I disagree."
The room gets very quiet.
Morpheus holds the silence for five full seconds.
Then something in him shifts.
Not softens — aligns.
Like a compass finding north.
"Tell me why," he says.
You tell him about the physics gap. About the forty milliseconds. About what it might mean if you understood why rather than just how.
He listens differently than he listened to anything else you've said.
When you finish, he doesn't respond immediately.
He looks at Tank.
Then at you.
"You are perhaps the first person," he says carefully, "who has ever argued with me about this and been right."
Tank loads a different program instead. A physics environment. No weapons. Just the world, stripped down, showing its seams.

choices.label: Study the seams. Then go to the rooftop.

text: You load the weapons.
They are useful. Efficient. The knowledge slots in like a key.
You are faster now. More capable.
But something feels borrowed that should feel earned.
Tank runs the download. Morpheus moves on.
The forty milliseconds sit in your notebook, unverified.
You don't stop thinking about them.
But you move faster.
Which is what they wanted.
Speed is not always the answer.
You will know that later.
You don't know it yet.
Two buildings.
The same gap they always use.
Morpheus is already on the far side.
You don't run.
You sit down.
At the edge of the roof, you sit down and study the horizon.
It bothers you.
Not the drop. The horizon.
It's not quite right. The curvature is wrong. The scale of the buildings in the distance — the falloff is too clean.
Like someone calculated the perspective but used the wrong formula.
Like whoever built this was very good at simulation but slightly unfamiliar with the actual world.

choices.label: Jump anyway. You've seen what you needed to see.

text: You stand.
You run.
You jump.
The gap is irrelevant.
You already know this place is not real.
Knowing that — actually knowing it, in your body — means the physics have to negotiate with you.
You land clean.
Morpheus looks at you differently now.
Not with the weight of a test administered.
With something that looks like relief.
You're ready to see the Oracle.
"Morpheus."
He crosses to your side of the gap. Easily. Like the gap is a suggestion.
"Look at the horizon. The scale is wrong."
He looks.
He is very still.
"What exactly did you see?"
You describe it. The falloff. The formula error. The way the furthest buildings are almost-but-not-quite the right size.
Morpheus is looking at you the way the Oracle looked at the vase.
Like something is about to fall and he already knows it.
"We need to get you to the Oracle," he says. "Now."
"I thought we were doing the jump."
"We can skip the jump."
Something in the way he says it makes you very certain he has never skipped the jump before.
The Oracle's building is in the Tenements.
Low and unassuming. The paint on the number above the door is the paint of something that has been repainted many times.
The neighborhood feels slightly wrong.
Not dangerous. Wrong.
Too clean in the wrong places. The graffiti stops exactly at the corner and starts again exactly at the next.
The people on the steps are the right kinds of people but in the wrong proportions.
Like someone designed a neighborhood from memory rather than observation.
You feel watched.
Not by Agents. By something older and less interested in stopping you.

choices.label: Log the patterns. Take a moment to look.

text: You stand on the sidewalk and look.
Really look.
The graffiti cutoff. The proportion of passersby. The way sound arrives from down the block a half-second late.
You take out nothing to write with. You write it all in your head, in the specific part of your memory that you've been training for exactly this.
Trinity is beside you.
"Neo."
"One second."
She watches you look at the neighborhood like it owes you something.
Then: "Okay."
You go inside.
But you carry it with you.
The neighborhood as evidence.
Evidence of what — you don't know yet.
Chairs along the wall. Other children, other potentials.
Some of them look at you. Most don't.
A boy in the far corner is bending a spoon.
Not bending — unbending. It curves away from him, returns, curves again.
He notices you watching.
He holds the spoon out toward you without standing up.
"Do not try to bend the spoon," he says. His voice is very steady for a child's voice. "That's impossible."
He pauses.
"Instead, only try to realize the truth."

choices.label: Pick up a spoon. Try to bend it.

text: "What truth?"
The boy turns the spoon over in his hands.
"There is no spoon," he says.
He says it simply, the way children state things that are true.
"Then you will see it is not the spoon that bends. It is only yourself."
He hands it to you.
It is a spoon.
It is entirely and completely a spoon.
You hold it.
You know what he means.
You can't do what he does.
But you know what he means.
That is not nothing.

choices.label: The Oracle calls for you. Go in.

text: You watch.
The spoon bends and unbends. The boy doesn't look at it.
He looks at you.
That's the thing you notice.
His attention isn't on the spoon at all.
His attention is on something inside himself. The spoon is just a report.
An output of something happening elsewhere.
The door at the end of the hall opens.
A woman leans out. She looks at you.
"Come on," she says. "I haven't got all day."
She goes back inside before you can answer.
You pick up a spoon from the tray beside you.
You hold it.
You think about what the boy said. There is no spoon. Only yourself.
You think about the door that glitched. The horizon that was wrong. The physics gap in the sparring program.
You think about twenty-six years in a pod.
The spoon bends.
Not dramatically. A centimeter. Maybe two.
The boy stares at you.
The other potentials look away quickly.
You set the spoon down.
Your hand is shaking slightly.
"Interesting," the boy says. He sounds like he means it.

choices.label: Go in. The door is open.

text: Cookies.
The smell arrives before everything else.
She is at the oven. She doesn't turn around right away.
The kitchen is warm. Specific in the way lived-in kitchens are specific — particular tile, particular crack in the ceiling, a calendar on the wall showing a month from three years ago.
She turns.
She looks at you.
Not at your face. At you.
The distinction is uncomfortable.
She looks for long enough that you want to fill the silence.
She looks for long enough that you decide not to.
"You're the Oracle."
She smiles. Slightly.
"And you're Neo. I know. Sit down."
She slides a plate of cookies across the table.
"You want to know if you're the One."
"Everyone does."
She sits down across from you.
The chair makes no sound.
"Here's the thing nobody tells you," she says. "You don't come here for answers. You come here to hear what you already know."
She folds her hands.
"So. What do you know?"

choices.label: Go to the Oracle's actual question. Continue.

text: She speaks first.
"Not quite what you expected."
"I didn't know what to expect."
"Sure you did. Everyone does." She sets a plate of cookies in front of you.
"Sit."
You sit.
"You have the gift," she says.
The word gift sounds clinical from her. Anatomical. Like she is describing a bone structure.
"But you're waiting for something."
"What am I waiting for?"
She looks at you with something that is not quite sympathy.
"Your next life," she says. "Maybe."

choices.label: Continue. Hear what she has to say.

text: She asks you a question.
She asks it easily, like the answer doesn't matter, which means the answer matters enormously.
"What do you think you are?"
The question sits in the warm kitchen air.
The cookies cool on the plate.
Outside, the neighborhood hums its slightly-wrong hum.
textPrompt: The Oracle is watching you. "What do you think you are?"

text: She listens to you.
When you finish she is quiet.
Then she stands and goes to the counter.
You don't know if you answered well or badly.
You're not sure those are the categories that apply here.
She says, with her back to you: "The interesting thing about that answer — it's yours. Not Morpheus's. Not mine."
"You're telling me I'm right?"
"I'm telling you it's yours." She pauses. "That's harder to come by than right."
Something shifts on the counter.
A vase.
She doesn't look at it.
"Don't worry about the vase," she says.
You look at the vase.
And you knock it over.
Not intentionally. Or maybe intentionally. You don't entirely know.
It falls.
It hits the floor.
It breaks.
Clean break. A vase that was, and now is ceramic shards and a memory of being a vase.
The Oracle looks at it.
"Sorry," you say.
"Oh, don't be." She picks up a piece, considers it.
"The real question is — would you have broken it if I hadn't said anything?"

choices.label: Say nothing. Think about the question.

text: She smiles.
Not a big smile. The specific smile of someone who has been waiting for that question specifically.
"What's really going to bake your noodle," she says, "is whether you would have broken it if I hadn't said anything."
She sweeps the pieces to one side with her foot.
"Causality is funny," she says. "Prediction and cause look identical from the outside."
"So you caused it."
"I mentioned it. You broke it. Language is a very complicated tool."
She pours tea.
She sets a cup in front of you.
You think about that for a moment.
You think about the simulation. About twenty-six years of being caused.
"Morpheus believes in you utterly," she says.
She says it without preamble. Just sets it on the table between you like the cookies.
"He'll sacrifice his life to save yours."
The kitchen is very warm.
"What do I do about that?"
She looks at you.
"That depends entirely on what you think you are."

choices.label: Say nothing. Just sit with it.

text: "You can't let that happen," you say.
She looks at you.
"I wasn't planning to stop it," she says quietly.
"Then I will."
She is quiet for a long time.
The cookies are done. She doesn't move to take them out.
"That," she says finally, "is either the most important thing you've said in this room. Or the most human."
"Maybe both."
"Maybe both," she agrees.
She stands.
"You already know what you need to do. You came here looking for permission."
A beat.
"You don't need it from me."
She looks at you for a long moment.
"You already know the answer to that," she says.
"Then tell me anyway."
She almost smiles.
"Go back. Watch what you haven't been watching." A pause. "There's someone on your crew who's already made a different deal."
She doesn't say his name.
She doesn't have to.
You feel it settle into place.
Something you felt but couldn't name.
Now it has a shape.
You say nothing.
The tea is getting cold.
She lets you sit.
After a while she says: "Most people try to talk their way past it."
"Past what?"
"The weight of it. Someone believing in you that completely."
She refills your cup.
"It's terrifying. Being believed in."
"Yes."
"More terrifying than not being believed in."
"Yes," you say again.
She nods.
Like you've said the exact right thing.
"Go back," she says gently. "Keep your eyes open."
The Nebuchadnezzar.
You come back through the jack and the ship feels different.
Not different. Your perception of it feels different. You are looking at it through the Oracle's kitchen, still.
Cypher is in the crew bay.
He's cleaning a gun.
He doesn't look up when you enter.
Most people look up.
The gun is already clean. Has been for a while, from the look of it.
He keeps cleaning it.

choices.label: Leave. Something is wrong and you don't know what yet.

text: "Hey."
He looks up.
There is nothing in his expression. That is the problem.
Cypher has always had an expression. Bitterness, mostly. A specific shrug at the world.
Tonight his face is blank.
People who have decided something have blank faces.
"Neo." He says your name like it's a code word he's been given to memorize.
"You okay?"
"Fine."
He goes back to the gun.
You stand in the doorway a moment longer.
Then you leave.
But you file it. The blankness. The gun. The fact that he didn't ask how the Oracle went.
He always asks how the Oracle goes.
You stand in the doorway and watch.
He keeps cleaning the gun.
His hands are shaking. Barely.
A tremor so small you'd miss it if you weren't looking for something wrong.
You're looking for something wrong.
And then: the smell.
Faint. Very faint. Something sweet that has no business being on a ship running on recyclable protein and grey water.
Something like cooked meat.
Something that smells, implausibly, expensively, like steak.
His hands stop moving.
He knows you're there.
He still doesn't look up.
You go to the upper deck.
You stand in the cold.
You think about what the Oracle said: someone on your crew has already made a different deal.
You think about the blank face on a man who is never blank.
You go back.
But this time you don't announce yourself.
Through the grating you can see him.
He's holding something to the light.
A data chip.
A data chip that is not standard issue.
A data chip that, from this angle, has a symbol on it you've never seen on this ship.
He tucks it away.
His hands are shaking.
Trinity is in the corridor near the jack bay.
She looks tired in the specific way she always looks tired — like she decided to be tired and is executing it efficiently.
She sees your face.
"How was the Oracle?"
"Interesting." You pause. "Trinity. Are you okay?"
Something moves across her face and then doesn't.
"Fine."
She says it the exact same way Cypher said it.
Two people saying fine.
Neither of them fine.

choices.label: "Tell me about Cypher. How long has he been on the crew?"

text: You tell her what you saw.
The blank face. The shaking hands. The smell of steak.
If you saw the chip, you tell her that too.
Trinity goes still.
Very still.
"How sure are you?"
"Not completely."
"But sure enough."
"Sure enough to tell you."
She doesn't move for a long moment.
Then: "I'll tell Morpheus."
"Quietly."
"Always quietly," she says.
There is something in her expression that is not quite relief.
More like: finally. Someone else sees it too.

choices.label: Go prepare for the jack-in. The oracle trip is over.

text: Trinity talks about Cypher.
Not much. She's not a person who talks much.
He's been on the crew six years. He was one of the first people Morpheus unplugged.
There was a time, she says, when he believed.
"What happened?"
"The real world happened," she says. "To him, the real world happened and it was worse than he thought it would be."
She pauses.
"Some people can hold the knowledge of what the world is and choose to stay. Some people can't."
She says it without judgment.
It sounds like something she's made peace with.
You wonder if she's made peace with it.

choices.label: Go prepare to jack back in.

text: The jack-in.
Cypher handles the connections.
His hands are steady now. Professionally steady.
He moves through the crew systematically. Trinity. Morpheus. The others.
When he gets to you he pauses.
His hands go to your headjack.
He plugs in the connector.
His hands linger.
A second longer than they should.
Maybe two.
You feel it — a slight pressure. A fraction of an extra second of his fingers near your skull.
Like he's saying something he doesn't have words for.
Or like he's saying goodbye.

choices.label: Let it go. He plugs you in.

text: You turn.
He is already looking somewhere else.
"What?" he says.
"Nothing."
You study his face.
His face gives you nothing.
"You ready?" he says.
"Yeah," you say.
He plugs you in.
The Matrix loads.
You carry with you into it the specific weight of that extra second.
Something happened on this ship.
Something that has a shape you can't quite see yet.
But you felt it.
That has to count for something.
The lobby.
Marble floors. High ceilings. The specific white noise of an institutional building in the middle of the day.
Six guards at the security station.
You and Trinity walk through the revolving door.
The glass is heavy. The air inside is conditioned to a precise temperature.
Everything is very clean.
Everything is very heavily armed.
Six guards. Two cameras. One metal detector.

choices.label: Go quiet. Take them down one by one.

text: You move along the columns.
Trinity takes the right. You take the left.
The first guard doesn't know anything is wrong until he's already on the floor.
The second turns. Sees you. Starts to shout.
Trinity is faster.
The third, fourth — efficiency. Clean. Professional.
The fifth is at the radio.
His hand is on it.
He's looking at you.
His thumb is moving toward the call button.

choices.label: Disarm him. Get the radio away first.
Neutralize fast. No time for precise.
Let him reach the radio. Handle what comes.

text: You move.
Your wrist catches his before his thumb connects.
The radio clatters to the marble.
He's down a second later.
The sixth guard by the elevator sees everything.
He is fast. He is also running toward the stairs, not away.
Trinity handles him before he makes the second step.
Silence.
Marble. Six bodies on the floor, breathing.
Trinity looks at you.
"Elevator."
"Elevator."
Fast.
He goes down before he can transmit.
The sixth guard sees.
Runs.
Trinity makes a sound — wordless, focused — and goes after him.
You hear a short struggle.
Silence.
The lobby is yours.
It took ninety seconds.
Trinity returns. Her lip is cut. She doesn't address it.
"Stairs or elevator?" she asks.
Her voice is completely flat.
He transmits.
You hear the click and then the static and then the voice coming back.
An alarm begins. Distant at first. Then the whole building.
The sixth guard is already drawing.
The next thirty seconds are very loud and very close.
When it's over the lobby is yours.
But the clock is running now.
"Elevator," Trinity says. She's breathing hard.
"Elevator."
Trinity draws.
You draw.
It is very loud in marble rooms.
The guards move well. They're trained for this.
But not for this.
Not for two people who have been downloaded with the kinetic memory of a hundred scenarios and who have nothing left to lose in a simulation they know is a simulation.
The lobby is yours in forty seconds.
The alarm started at fifteen.
Trinity hotwires the elevator with one hand and holds the door with the other.
"Thirty seconds," Tank says in your ear. "Before they route an Agent."

choices.label: A guard gets close. Handle it physically.

text: The guard is close. Four feet. Weapon raised.
You don't give him the shot.
You close the distance instead.
That's the move they don't expect.
Four feet becomes zero feet and then he's on the floor.
Trinity has the elevator.
"Go," she says.
You go.
The doors close behind you.
The lobby recedes.
You breathe.
"Hold on," you say.
He stops. Barely.
"Listen. Listen to me for five seconds."
He looks at you the way people look at things that don't make sense but are undeniably happening.
Four seconds is enough.
Trinity has the elevator.
"Thank you," you say.
You mean it, vaguely.
You get in the elevator.
The doors close.
Trinity looks at you.
"Did you just politely distract a guard?"
"It worked."
She makes a sound that is almost a laugh.
Almost.
The upper floor.
The elevator opens on a hallway that smells like carpet cleaner and institutional nothing.
There are choices here.
The stairs continue up. The elevator is logged now.
If flags.physicsGap and flags.trainingSlow — you can see it.
The floor plan has a pattern. The guards on this floor move on a rotation you could plot.
You can see it the way you see code.
The forty millisecond gap, applied to building security.
There is a route through that avoids contact entirely.

choices.label: Take the stairs. Slower, flanking angles.
Move through the code. Route around them entirely.

text: The stairs.
Concrete. Echoing.
You move floor by floor.
Trinity takes the inside. You take the outside.
On the nineteenth floor there is a guard who is awake and paying attention.
He sees you at the same moment you see him.
Eleven seconds of close-quarters problem-solving.
You reach the thirty-first floor breathing hard.
Morpheus is behind a heavy door.
Two guards in the hall.
Trinity looks at you.
You look at Trinity.
You've done this before. Just now, in the lobby. You know how you each move.
You see it.
The rotation.
Guard A pauses at the east window every four minutes, seven seconds.
Guard B's route has a seventeen-second gap at the northwest corner.
The camera on the second junction has a blind spot.
You explain it to Trinity in twenty words.
She listens. Nods once.
You move through the building like water finding the lowest path.
No one sees you.
No one hears you.
You reach the thirty-first floor unmarked.
Trinity looks at you like you've done something that shouldn't be possible.
"How did you—"
"Physics," you say.
She doesn't push it.
Smith catches your strike.
Not deflects. Catches.
His hand closes around your fist and the grip is wrong.
Too tight. Too fast. The physics of the hand tightening beyond what pressure the simulation should generate.
You notice.
Even in the pain of it, you notice.
He is not human. He is not pretending to be human.
He is something else wearing a human's face because human faces are what the system provided.
"Mr. Anderson," he says.
He says your old name like it's a diagnosis.

choices.label: Fight through it. Don't stop moving.
Understand what you're feeling. The grip is data.

text: You fight.
He is faster than you.
He is stronger than you.
He hits you three times in sequences that shouldn't be physically possible.
You hit the ground each time.
You get up each time.
That seems to bother him.
Not the getting-up. The not-staying-down.
"Why," he says, "do you persist?"
He asks it the way programs ask questions.
Like he's logging the answer.
Like he doesn't understand the variable.
The grip is data.
Too tight. Physics violation. The simulation generating force beyond its own rules.
Which means the rules are not fixed.
Which means the rules were never fixed.
Which means—
He releases you suddenly.
You step back.
Smith tilts his head.
"Interesting," he says.
He says it the way file systems say corrupted.
"You are not reacting the way you should."
"How should I react?"
"With fear."
You look at him.
"I know what you are," you say.

choices.label: Hold that ground. Let him come to you.

text: You stop.
Smith stops.
The simulation has a sound you've never noticed before.
A very low hum. Constant. Like something enormous breathing.
Smith looks at you.
He has never had an opponent go still before.
The variable is unexpected.
His grip tightens and then — doesn't.
He releases you.
Cautious.
Like stillness is a weapon he doesn't have a counter for.
Maybe it is.

choices.label: Listen to the monologue. Let him talk.

text: "You are a virus, Mr. Anderson."
He says it not with anger.
With disgust.
The specific revulsion of a system encountering something it was not designed to process.
"You move to an area and you multiply. You consume every natural resource. You spread to another area."
He looks at you the way something looks at something it cannot classify.
"There is another organism on this planet that follows the same pattern. Do you know what it is?"
He means it.
He genuinely means it.
This is not a performance.
This is a conclusion.
"You're a program."
Smith pauses.
"Yes," he says. He sounds almost bored. "And you are code with a heartbeat. Which of us is more real?"
You think about that.
You have thought about that.
You think about twenty-six years in a pod and what real means when applied to experience.
"I am," you say.
Smith stares.
"Conviction," he says.
Not a question.
"That is what separates you from your kind."
He comes at you.
You say nothing.
Smith finishes his sentence into your silence.
The silence bothers him.
More than a reply would have.
Arguments require two participants.
You have declined to participate.
His jaw tightens.
"You should be afraid," he says.
"I know," you say.
He comes at you.
You're already moving.
You laugh.
Not much. Not theatre.
A short, involuntary sound.
Smith stops.
Something moves across his face that you have never seen on a program's face before.
Something that looks almost like — uncertainty.
The laugh doesn't fit his model.
He pauses to recalculate.
That pause is two seconds.
Two seconds that you don't waste.
You move while he's still deciding what to do with laughter.
The bullet finds you.
Not all of them. One.
It burns. A specific burning that is very different from the burning in training.
This burning has weight.
You are still standing.
You don't know why you are still standing.
You look down.
Then you look at Smith's face.
Smith's face is surprised.
He hit you and you are still standing.
That is not the behavior his model predicts.
Something cracks in his expression.
Very small.
A crack.

choices.label: Look at the wound. Understand it.
Look at Smith's face. Study that crack.

text: You look down.
Blood. Real enough.
But the pain is already changing.
Receding to something manageable.
Your body knows something your mind doesn't.
The wound is real and it is also code.
And you are something that sits between real and code, in the space that has no good name.
You look up.
Smith is watching you understand this.
"What are you doing?" he says.
Not a taunt. A genuine question.
Even programs can be surprised.
You look at his face.
The crack is there.
Just for a moment.
A program that encountered something outside its parameters.
He is not invulnerable.
He is not inevitable.
He is very fast and very strong and operating within a system that was designed to contain you.
But the system has cracks.
He has cracks.
And you can see them.
You start to understand — not yet fully, not yet completely — what that means.
"Believe it," you say.
He doesn't understand.
You barely understand.
But the word lands.
You run.
Down the corridor. Stairs. Another hallway.
The phone is one floor below. Ringing.
An Agent moves to cut off the stairwell.
Ten feet between you and the corner.
Trinity is on the line: "Neo. One floor down. The room on the right."
An Agent is between you and the room.
The phone is ringing.
It is very loud.
It is the only sound that matters.

choices.label: Fight through. Get to the phone.
Find another way. Window. Any exit.

text: The window.
You don't think about it.
Glass and then air and then the fire escape.
Metal under your feet. The city below. Real wind.
The Agent reaches the window behind you.
He looks at you on the fire escape.
You look at him.
Then you move.
Down two flights. In the window on the second floor. The hallway.
The room on the right.
The phone.
Still ringing.
You answer it.
You stop.
The hallway goes quiet in a way that hallways don't.
Smith stops.
The Agent behind him stops.
The simulation hesitates.
You look at your hands.
They are code.
You can see it — not the green waterfall from training, not the glimpse through the glitching door.
The actual structure.
The foundation.
You are standing inside an argument about reality.
And you are the argument.
You are not in the Matrix.
The Matrix is in you.
Has always been in you.
You are the one thing it could never fully simulate.
The decision to know.
You raise your hand.
Smith moves.
Three Agents move.
The bullets move.
And then they don't.
They hang in the air between you like questions waiting for an answer.
You have never done this.
You didn't know this was possible.
You don't move them.
You just don't let them continue.
The distinction is everything.
You are not overriding the system.
You are refusing to be overridden by it.
Smith stares.
The Agents stare.
The simulation processes.
The simulation has no response prepared.
"I know what this is," you say.
The words land differently in this hallway than words have ever landed.
Smith tilts his head.
"You are a process," you say. "Running on a system. Following parameters you were given by people who are afraid of what I am."
You can feel it — the simulation around you adjusting.
Not complying.
Adjusting.
Negotiating.
With you.
"What you are," Smith says slowly, "is impossible."
"I know," you say.
"Then how—"
"Because impossible is a rule," you say. "And I've read your rules. I know where they gap."
Smith moves.
You are faster.
Smith is silent.
For the first time in this entire conversation.
In all the conversations before this one.
He is looking at you the way a system looks at a variable it has no category for.
The bullets are still hanging in the air.
The Agents have not moved.
The simulation is waiting for you to decide what comes next.
For the first time, you understand what Morpheus meant.
What the Oracle knew.
You don't believe because they told you.
You believe because you stopped waiting to be told.
"It's over," you say.
Smith looks at you.
He says nothing.
Maybe the nothing is agreement.
Maybe it's the closest a program can come to grief.
The bullets fall.
Softly. Like something that was never dangerous.
Like rain.
You walk past Smith.
He doesn't stop you.
The phone is ringing somewhere below.
But you already know what this is.
You already know what comes next.
Not because someone told you.
Because you have, finally, chosen to know.
You walk past him.
Smith does not move.
The Agents do not move.
The hallway is very long.
You walk it.
You don't run.
Running is for something chasing you.
Nothing is chasing you.
Not anymore.
At the end of the hallway there is a door.
You open it.
Light.
Real light. A quality of light that no simulation has quite managed.
You walk toward it.
The city cracks open.
You see the code.
All of it.
Everyone.
Every wire and breath and choice.
You see it.
And you are no longer afraid of what you see.
You look him in the eye.
Green. The wrong green — the green of something that was always code.
He looks back.
There is something in there that might be, in a program's architecture, the closest equivalent to understanding.
Not acceptance.
Understanding.
"You knew," he says.
"Not at first."
"But you know now."
"Yes."
He tilts his head.
"Then why are you still here?"
You think about that.
"Because you needed to see it too," you say.
You leave him to whatever a program does when it has encountered something outside its parameters.
You walk toward the phone.
The city is already changing around you.
You can see every seam.
None of them hold you anymore.
